segments.text: [Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Opener, The pigments in butterfly wings are based on a class of nitrogen-rich heterocylic compounds called pteridines. In fact, pteridines are named after the Greek pteron (wing). Pteridine is also a component of folic acid, a central coenzyme in amino acid metabolism.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.1, Pathways of nitrogen metabolism (highlighted in color) in the general pattern of intermediary metabolism.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.2, Relationships between inorganic and organic nitrogen metabolism.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.3, The site of nitrogen fixation in symbiotic root nodules.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.4, Structure of molybdenum-dependent nitrogenase from Azotobacter vinelandii.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.5, Reactions in assimilation of ammonia and major fates of the fixed nitrogen.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.6, Structure of the human proteasome.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.7, Involvement of pyridoxal phosphate in transamination.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.8, Tetrahydrofolate carries activated one-carbon units for biosynthesis.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.9, Structure of vitamin B12.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.10, Transport of ammonia to the liver for urea synthesis.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.11, The Krebs–Henseleit urea cycle.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.12, Fates of the amino acid carbon skeletons.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.13, Alanine, cysteine, glycine, serine, and threonine are degraded to pyruvate in mammals.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.14, Branched-chain amino acid oxidation, fatty acid β-oxidation, and the citric acid cycle share a common chemical strategy.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.15, The phenylalanine hydroxylation system.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.16, Catabolism of phenylalanine and tyrosine to fumarate and acetoacetate.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Table 18.1, Nutritional requirements for amino acids in mammals
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.17, Amino acid carbon skeletons (green) derive from intermediates of glycolysis (blue), the citric acid cycle (gray),  or the pentose phosphate pathway (orange).
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.18, Synthesis of alanine, aspartate, glutamate, asparagine, and glutamine.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.19, Metabolic interconversions and fates of serine and glycine.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.20, Biosynthesis of isoleucine, leucine, and valine.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.21, Methyl group metabolism and homocystinuria.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.22, DNA sequencing reveals a common polymorphism in human methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHFR).
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.23, Biosynthesis of nitric oxide and creatine phosphate from arginine.
[Speaker Notes] Chapter 18, Figure 18.24, Biosynthesis of the catecholamines—dopamine, norepinephrine,  and epinephrine—from tyrosine.
